label: U.S. Marriage Certificate
description: A marriage certificate records the names of both parties as they were at the time of the ceremony. In most U.S. states, this record cannot be amended to reflect a later legal name change — it is a historical record of what was true on that date.
immutable_note: A marriage certificate is a contemporaneous record of a legal event. In most U.S. states, it cannot be amended to reflect names that were not in effect at the time of the marriage.
immutable_compassion_note: This is one of the documents where the law simply has not caught up. Your marriage happened, it was real, and the certificate remains valid — it just reflects your name as it was at that moment. Many agencies have ways to work with this, and you are not without options.
workarounds: Present your marriage certificate alongside your court order for legal name change. Most agencies and institutions accept this combination as proof that both names belong to the same person.

Some states allow a name amendment to be attached to a marriage certificate as a separate order — this does not alter the original certificate, but creates an official link between the two names. Check your state's vital records office.
For passport applications and similar documents, a combination of your marriage certificate plus a court order (or updated ID) is the standard approach when the names differ.

presence_level_content.some_guidance: Agencies that ask for a marriage certificate alongside a name-changed ID are familiar with this situation. Presenting both documents together is the accepted practice.
presence_level_content.walk_with_me: It can feel strange that a document about your marriage does not reflect your name. Some people find it helps to think of it as a record of a day — not a definition of who you are now.
